1. Representation theory of finite groups

1.1. Motivation and Definition

  • A finite group can be described in terms of its presentation: a set of generators and relations among those generators. For example,

    (1)

    From these presentations it is not obvious that . But from looking at the actions (permuting vertices of a triangle) it becomes visualizable.

  • Eventually actions become unwieldy and we want more structure. Describing them as linear actions lets us have notions of decomposition, eigenvalue, orthogonality. Hence we obtain a systematic framework—unavailable at the level of presentations or set actions alone—that we can apply to Fourier analysis and equivariant neural networks.

  • The idea of R.T. is to realize groups as groups of linear operations. For example we have the standard representation of :

    (2)

    This defines a homomorphism .

Definition 1. A complex representation of on is a homomorphism

(3)

The dimension of a representation is said to be

  • Examples:

    4. (Regular representation) Let be a vector space of dimension with basis elements for all .

      (4)

      Each is simply a permutation matrix.

Remarks.

  • Sometimes we will abuse notation and let
  • A group representation can be viewed as a group action on (homomorphism from to ) that is also linear.

Definition 2 (Equivariance). Suppose and are two representations of . A linear map is a -homomorphism if it commutes with all elements of :

(5)

We also say is equivariant to .

Example 1. Equivariance is of interest in machine learning for tasks where we want the model output to transform in the same way as its input. E.g. for pixel classification, if we reflect the input image the output should be also reflected. See ml.typ for more.

is a -isomorphism if is invertible, so that we can write

(6)

One goal of representation theory is to find all representations of a group up to isomorphism.

1.2. Irreps

Definition 3. is irreducible if the only -invariant subspaces of are and itself.

Example: Any -dimensional representation is irreducible. Kernel and image of an equivariant map are -invariant.

Lemma 1 (Schur's Lemma). Suppose and are irreducible representations of .

  1. Suppose . Then either or is a -isomorphism.
  2. Suppose . Then is a scalar operator, i.e.
Proof
  1. Casework on whether is trivial, and apply the definition of irreducibility.
  2. Take an eigenvalue of . Apply (1) to show that .

□

Later we will see that the following converse of Schur’s lemma holds from Maschke’s theorem:

Lemma 2 (Schur's Lemma, Converse). Suppose is reducible. Then

Remark. The existence of an equivariant map implies that the two irreps are isomorphic.

Lemma 3. Any irreducible representation of a finite group is of dimension .

1.3. Complete Reducibility

Consider a -invariant subspace . Since is a linear map, we have , and we can consider the subrepresentation of restricted to . Notably, if is the direct sum of two -invariant subspaces, the matrix of with respect to their basis is block diagonal, and we can define a direct sum of representations:

(7)

Can all reducible representations can be decomposed like this (called completely reducible)? Yes for finite groups (Maschke’s theorem) but additional assumptions on are required for infinite group.

Counterexample for infinite groups

Let , and define a 2D representation by

(8)

This doesn’t contradict Peter-Weyl since is not compact.

Theorem 1 (Maschke's Theorem). Every representation of a finite group on over a field with characteristic not dividing the order of is a direct sum of irreducible representations.

Corollary: Every finite group has an irreducible representation.

Before proving Maschke’s Theorem, we introduce the notation of unitary representations.

Let be a positive definite Hermitian form on . Recall that is a unitary operator if

(9)

Definition 4. We say is a unitary representation if has a positive definite Hermitian form such that

(10)

Equivalently, there exists a change of basis such that is a unitary matrix1 for all .

Proof of equivalence

Suppose there exists such a unitary form. Then we have for some Hermitiain positive-definite matrix . Then take the Cholesky decomposition , so that in the new basis , the representation becomes , and

For the reverse proof, we can construct using the change of basis. □

As a consequence of the second definition, each can be diagonalized according to some basis that depends on .

Proof of Maschke's Theorem
Suppose is not irreducible. Then there exists a nonproper -invariant . Thus we can express as a direct sum with respect to and . Then we can apply strong induction2 (base case: any -dimensional representation is irreducible)

Remark. A stronger form of the theorem states that is completely reducible if and only if the characteristic of does not divide .

Corollary. Suppose is finite abelian. Since commuting unitary operators have a common eigenbasis, every irreducible representation of is 1-dimensional.

We will see another way to prove the above corollary with the Main Theorem.

Lemma 6. The operator on defined as for operators and has trace .

Proof is just looking at coefficient of for each elementary matrix in .

Proof of (2). So far we know the characters span a subspace of class functions. To show that it spans the entire space, it suffices to show that the only class function orthogonal to every character is the zero function. Apply Artin Lemma 10.8.5: which uses the regular representation to prove that the only class function orthogonal to every character is the zero function.

Corollary. (1) and (2) imply that the characters of isomorphism classes of irreducible representations form an orthonormal basis of class functions.

1.5. Induced Representations

How can we build representations of a bigger group from representations of a subgroup in a universal manner?

Frobenius reciprocity allows us to compute maps into or out of an induced representation in by staying in .
